question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the operator key is loaded from HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY without any key format or strength validation beyond hiero.PrivateKeyFromString() parsing, allowing a weak or known-compromised key to be used to authorize transfers via Execute(client) without the true account owner's consent?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that submitWithRetry() does not check whether the operator account has sufficient hbar balance before calling Execute(client), causing the transaction to be submitted and potentially partially processed (fee deducted) without the operator explicitly approving a low-balance transfer attempt?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not implement any rate-limiting or per-transfer approval gate, so that by setting HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL to a very small value, Execute(client) is called at an attacker-controlled rate, authorizing an unbounded number of transfers without the operator approving each one individually?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero SDK's Execute(client) method automatically signs the transaction with the operator key without requiring an explicit .Sign() call, meaning there is no code path in submitWithRetry() where a human or secondary system can intercept and approve/reject the transaction before it is authorized on-chain?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that submitWithRetry() does not log the amount or destination before calling Execute(client), so that if cfg is tampered with between ticks, a transfer to an attacker-controlled account for an attacker-controlled amount is authorized and only logged after the fact, when it is too late to prevent?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger process writes /tmp/ready after newClient() succeeds but before any transfer authorization check, so that a Kubernetes readiness probe passes even if the operator key is invalid or belongs to a different account, masking a misconfiguration that could lead to unauthorized transfer attempts?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that Execute(client) in submitWithRetry() does not call .SetRegenerateTransactionID(false), allowing the SDK to regenerate the transaction ID on retry, which means each retry attempt is a new authorization of the same transfer without the operator explicitly approving each retry?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not implement any approval workflow (e.g., a confirmation step or a second-factor check) before Execute(client) is called, meaning any process that can set the environment variables can authorize arbitrary transfers from the operator account without the account owner's interactive approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.AccountIDFromString(cfg.toAccountID) does not verify that toID is a valid, active, non-deleted account on-chain before Execute(client) is called, allowing the attacker to set toAccountID to a deleted or recycled account ID and have the transfer authorized to an unintended recipient?
